Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time, but then I'd get distracted by other stuff—my extracurriculars, hanging out with friends, whatever. I learn best by doing things hands-on, so sitting down to read or watch lectures felt like a struggle. Most weeks I'd end up cramming things in whenever I could find a spare moment rather than having any real structure.
Q2: It definitely varied a lot based on deadlines. If something was due soon, I'd suddenly be all over it, but otherwise I'd drift and let days go by without touching course stuff. My weeks were unpredictable with my activities and social life, so regular study times just never stuck. I kept telling myself I'd get more organized, but it never really happened.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or getting a notification that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which happened more than I'd like to admit. Occasionally a friend would mention something from class and I'd realize I was behind, so I'd jump on to catch up. It wasn't really a habit, more like reactive.
Q4: For a little while, yeah, I got into a rhythm of checking in after my morning coffee before heading out. That lasted maybe two or three weeks when things felt calmer. But then life got busy again and the routine fell apart. It's weird—when I did make it a habit, I actually felt less anxious, but I couldn't sustain it.
Q5: The videos were actually pretty helpful because I could pause and rewind when I zoned out, which happened a lot. Discussion posts were good too because seeing what other people thought helped things click for me. I liked anything interactive or where I could actually do something rather than just passively read. That hands-on element made it feel more real.
Q6: The reading materials and long PDFs—I barely touched those. My brain just doesn't work that way; I need movement and engagement, not walls of text. I'd download them thinking I'd read them later, but later never came. Some of the supplementary stuff also felt optional, so I just skipped it when I was stressed for time.
Q7: If I could see how it connected to an assignment or the exam, I'd actually do it. Anything that felt practical or like it would help me pass made the cut. But if something seemed like busywork or extra fluff, I'd scroll right past. I needed to feel like my time investment would pay off somehow.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt like a suggestion I could ignore, especially when I was already overwhelmed. Required activities had that pressure behind them, so I'd force myself through even when I didn't want to. Optional things got sacrificed first when my anxiety spiked and I had to triage what to focus on. It's not that I didn't see their value—I just couldn't handle everything.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then study from my phone or laptop offline, especially when I was out or didn't have good wifi. I also used YouTube a lot to find explanations that made more sense to me. Sometimes I'd work through problems with friends in person, talking things out rather than staring at a screen.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours with a friend going over concepts, drawing diagrams, working through practice problems together—none of that showed up anywhere. Or I'd be pacing around my room listening to downloaded lectures, which is how I actually absorb stuff. The LMS just saw me as inactive, but I was genuinely learning in my own way.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a video once, download it or screenshot the key parts, and never go back to the LMS version. Same with slides—I'd grab them early in the week and study from my downloads. It was easier to access offline and I didn't have to deal with slow loading or logging in repeatedly.
Q12: Around midterms, everything piled up and I just hit a wall. My anxiety was through the roof, and I felt paralyzed—like I had so much to do that I couldn't start anything. There were also personal things going on that drained my energy. The mindfulness stuff they introduced actually helped me get through that rough patch, surprisingly.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars meant I was constantly juggling, and the course often lost that battle. Confusion about what was actually important also made me waste time on the wrong things sometimes.
Q14: Yeah, there were nights I'd have the LMS open for hours but my brain was elsewhere. I'd click through videos on 2x speed just to mark them as watched, not really absorbing anything. It felt like going through the motions to check a box rather than actually learning. My mind was too scattered to focus properly.
Q15: I put off the bigger assignments constantly because they felt overwhelming. The anxiety would build up and instead of tackling it, I'd avoid it and do smaller, easier tasks to feel productive. I knew it was making things worse, but starting felt impossible sometimes. It's like my brain would rather do anything else than face the hard stuff.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd be ghost mode until a few days before something was due, then suddenly I'm online constantly, scrambling. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the only thing that got me moving. Without deadlines, I'd probably never submit anything—they were my external motivation since my internal motivation was unreliable.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did motivate me to engage more with the next thing. Positive comments made me feel like maybe I wasn't as behind as I thought. But feedback often came too late to really change my approach for that assignment. I wished there was more immediate feedback so I could adjust in real-time.
Q18: The structure was okay, but it felt a bit rigid for someone like me who learns better through doing. Having set modules helped me know what was expected, but I sometimes felt trapped by the linear progression. I would've engaged more if there were more flexible, hands-on options built into the design.
Q19: The video content being broken into shorter chunks helped a lot—I could handle 15 minutes way better than an hour-long lecture. But some of the written instructions were confusing, which made me anxious about whether I was doing things right. Clearer guidance and more interactive elements would've made engagement easier for sure.
Q20: I think that's someone who's trying but struggling to convert activity into output. Maybe they're confused about what's expected, or they're dealing with stuff outside of class that makes it hard to finish things. High activity could also be someone anxiously checking and rechecking without making progress—I've been there.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I did a lot of studying offline or with friends that never showed up in the system. You can understand material without constantly being logged in. Some people are just efficient or learn better through methods that don't involve clicking around the LMS all day.
Q22: Logs can show when you're online and what you clicked, but they can't show if you actually understood anything. They miss all the offline work, the conversations with classmates, the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They also can't capture your emotional state—whether you're engaged or just going through the motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning, not just completing tasks to pass. It's when the material connects to something meaningful and you want to understand it deeper. For me, engagement happens when I can do something with the knowledge, apply it somehow. It's more than just showing up—it's being mentally present.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety throughout the course, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope. It wouldn't capture the late-night study sessions with friends or the times I learned something by physically working through it. My engagement was real, just not always visible in the data.
